data.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion A1 (anti-slop / over-engineering / NIH). Findings + affirmations below.

AFFIRMATIONS (A1 passes):
- YAGNI: the disable-config + rollback path serve a current done-definition (the T12 gap the gate flagged on itself), not a hypothetical — the gate introduces a real blocking change to the in-use claim path, so an off-switch is a present need, not speculative.
- No golden-hammer / resume-driven / premature-optimization: scope is doc/process-oriented (a config toggle + a written rollback doc + a spec cross-reference); no trendy tech, no speculative abstraction, no perf optimization without evidence.
- NIH (no rebuild of a 3rd-party dependency): the disable mechanism reuses the project's own typed config layer (src/rebar/config.py load_config), not a new framework.

FINDINGS (one checklist line each):

- [ ] Confirm the "disable-the-gate config" reuses the gate's existing enable-toggle (its natural inverse) rather than adding a *separate* disable key — a redundant second flag is config-surface proliferation.
  criteria: A1
  location: Scope — "A project-level disable-the-gate config (gate off => claim behaves exactly as today; fail-open)" / AC line 1
  evidence: The gate is already described as "config-gated and fail-open when off" (docs/experiments/plan-review-finalize-and-threepass.md:114-117: "the gate is config-gated and fail-open when off, so disabling it via config IS the kill-switch / rollback"). If an enable-flag already exists, "off" already IS the kill-switch and a NEW disable key duplicates it. The established sibling pattern is verify.require_signature_for_close (src/rebar/_commands/txn.py:251, config.py), a single boolean default-OFF — the gate's toggle should mirror that one key, not add a second.
  suggested_fix: Reword AC1 to assert the gate's existing on/off config key, set OFF, makes claim unchanged + fail-open — rather than introducing a distinct "disable" flag.

- [ ] Reconcile AC1's "fail-open when off" with the codebase's deliberate fail-CLOSED-on-unreadable-config convention so the toggle isn't cargo-culted into a less-safe shape than its sibling.
  criteria: A1
  location: AC line 1 — "claim is unchanged (fail-open) when off"
  evidence: The directly-analogous close gate fails CLOSED when config is present-but-unreadable (src/rebar/_commands/txn.py:263-278: "a present-but-unreadable config must never silently disable the gate — require a signature"; tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_close_gate_story_epic.py:136). "Fail-open" here means "gate-off ⇒ behave as today," which is fine, but the plan should be explicit that this refers to the disabled-by-config case, not the unreadable-config case — otherwise it copies the toggle shape without the understood safety rule.
  suggested_fix:

- [ ] Confirm the rollback/back-out + old/new-coexistence design genuinely needs a NEW documented mechanism vs. cross-referencing the existing event-rollout convention the plan already cites.
  criteria: A1
  location: Scope — "old+new coexistence during rollout (older clones preserve-and-ignore the new events — cross-check with the TAG_DELTA/event-rollout note)" / AC line 2
  evidence: The plan itself already points at an existing "TAG_DELTA/event-rollout note" for preserve-and-ignore semantics. If that convention already governs old/new clone coexistence, the AC should consume it (reference + reuse) rather than author a parallel coexistence scheme — otherwise it risks rebuilding an in-repo convention (NIH). Whether TAG_DELTA fully covers the REVIEW_RESULT-sidecar + signature events is the load-bearing fact; it is not determinable from the plan text alone.
  suggested_fix: Have AC2 state the rollback doc REUSES the TAG_DELTA/event-rollout preserve-and-ignore convention for coexistence, and only adds the deploy-ordering specifics for the sidecar+signature events.
